Curriculum → AI Engineer learning path → Machine learning foundations → Classical ML algorithms

Lesson 2 of 3

Classical ML algorithms

Regression, classification, clustering, and ensemble methods using scikit-learn.

30-40 min AI/ML flow Machine learning foundations 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

Classical algorithms remain the best choice for many production problems and serve as baselines against which deep learning must justify its complexity.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Supervised learning Linear models are interpretable and fast; start here as a baseline2. Unsupervised learning K-means is simple but assumes spherical clusters of similar size3. Feature engineering and selection One-hot encoding, binning, and interaction features expand model capacity

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing Classical algorithms remain the best choice for many production problems and serve as baselines against which deep learning must justify its complexity. 2. Supervised learning Linear models are interpretable and fast; start here as a baseline3. Unsupervised learning K-means is simple but assumes spherical clusters of similar size4. Feature engineering and selection One-hot encoding, binning, and interaction features expand model capacity
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

Classical algorithms remain the best choice for many production problems and serve as baselines against which deep learning must justify its complexity.

What to say first

  • Can select an appropriate algorithm family given the problem type and data shape.
  • Knows when to prefer tree ensembles over linear models.
  • Understands the bias-variance tradeoff in model selection.
2

Study section

Supervised learning

Linear regression, logistic regression, decision trees, random forests, gradient boosting, and SVMs cover the majority of tabular prediction tasks.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Linear models are interpretable and fast; start here as a baseline
  • Tree ensembles (XGBoost, LightGBM) dominate tabular data competitions
  • SVMs work well in high-dimensional spaces with clear margins

Code example

Train and compare classifiers with scikit-learn

from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier, GradientBoostingClassifier

X, y = load_iris(return_X_y=True)

models = {
    "Logistic Regression": LogisticRegression(max_iter=200),
    "Random Forest":       RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100),
    "Gradient Boosting":   GradientBoostingClassifier(n_estimators=100),
}

for name, model in models.items():
    scores = cross_val_score(model, X, y, cv=5, scoring='accuracy')
    print(f"{name:22s}  acc={scores.mean():.3f} ± {scores.std():.3f}")
3

Study section

Unsupervised learning

K-means, DBSCAN, hierarchical clustering, and dimensionality reduction reveal structure in unlabeled data.

Call these points out explicitly

  • K-means is simple but assumes spherical clusters of similar size
  • DBSCAN finds arbitrarily shaped clusters and handles noise
  • PCA and t-SNE reduce dimensions for visualization and denoising
4

Study section

Feature engineering and selection

Transforming raw data into informative features often matters more than algorithm choice.

Call these points out explicitly

  • One-hot encoding, binning, and interaction features expand model capacity
  • Feature selection reduces overfitting and speeds training
  • Domain knowledge drives the most impactful feature ideas

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Can select an appropriate algorithm family given the problem type and data shape.
  • Knows when to prefer tree ensembles over linear models.
  • Understands the bias-variance tradeoff in model selection.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Using deep learning on small tabular datasets where gradient boosting wins.
  • Neglecting feature engineering in favor of complex architectures.
  • Not establishing a simple baseline before moving to advanced methods.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • When would you choose logistic regression over a neural network?
  • Explain the bias-variance tradeoff with a concrete example.
  • How does gradient boosting differ from random forests?

Related topics

2 code examples 0 design drills

What to master

Study this in your own words

  • Can select an appropriate algorithm family given the problem type and data shape.
  • Knows when to prefer tree ensembles over linear models.
  • Understands the bias-variance tradeoff in model selection.

Production lens

Failure modes to call out

  • Using deep learning on small tabular datasets where gradient boosting wins.
  • Neglecting feature engineering in favor of complex architectures.
  • Not establishing a simple baseline before moving to advanced methods.

Practice aloud

Questions worth rehearsing

  • When would you choose logistic regression over a neural network?
  • Explain the bias-variance tradeoff with a concrete example.
  • How does gradient boosting differ from random forests?

Code example

Model selection for real-world datasets

intermediate coding

Given three datasets (small tabular, high-dimensional text, time-series), choose and train appropriate models using scikit-learn. Compare performance using proper cross-validation.

What to notice

  • Use cross_val_score with cv=5 for reliable estimates
  • Try linear models, tree ensembles, and SVMs
  • Small datasets often favor simpler models that don't overfit

Expected outcome

Comparison showing gradient boosting or random forest likely winning on this small dataset

Starter code 
from sklearn.datasets import make_classification, make_regression
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier, GradientBoostingClassifier
import numpy as np

# Dataset: 200 samples, 20 features, 5 informative
X, y = make_classification(n_samples=200, n_features=20, n_informative=5, random_state=42)

# TODO: Train at least 3 different models
# TODO: Use 5-fold cross-validation to compare
# TODO: Report mean and std of accuracy for each
# Which model wins and why?
Reference solution 
from sklearn.datasets import make_classification
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier, GradientBoostingClassifier
from sklearn.svm import SVC
import numpy as np

X, y = make_classification(n_samples=200, n_features=20, n_informative=5, random_state=42)

models = {
    'Logistic Regression': LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000),
    'Random Forest': RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42),
    'Gradient Boosting': GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=42),
    'SVM (RBF)': SVC(kernel='rbf')
}

for name, model in models.items():
    scores = cross_val_score(model, X, y, cv=5, scoring='accuracy')
    print(f"{name}: {scores.mean():.3f} (+/- {scores.std():.3f})")

Code example

Build a feature engineering pipeline

intermediate coding

Create a scikit-learn Pipeline that handles missing values, scales numeric features, one-hot encodes categoricals, and trains a model.

What to notice

  • ColumnTransformer applies different transforms to different column groups
  • Use SimpleImputer with strategy="median" for numeric, "most_frequent" for categorical
  • Pipeline ensures preprocessing is applied consistently in train and predict

Expected outcome

A fitted pipeline that handles all preprocessing and prediction in one call

Starter code 
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, OneHotEncoder
from sklearn.impute import SimpleImputer
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

# Sample data with mixed types and missing values
data = pd.DataFrame({
    'age': [25, 30, np.nan, 45, 35],
    'salary': [50000, 60000, 75000, np.nan, 55000],
    'city': ['NYC', 'LA', 'NYC', 'SF', 'LA'],
    'target': [0, 1, 1, 0, 1]
})

# TODO: Build a ColumnTransformer that:
# 1. Imputes and scales numeric features (age, salary)
# 2. One-hot encodes categorical features (city)
# TODO: Wrap in a Pipeline with a classifier
Reference solution 
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, OneHotEncoder
from sklearn.impute import SimpleImputer
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

data = pd.DataFrame({
    'age': [25, 30, np.nan, 45, 35],
    'salary': [50000, 60000, 75000, np.nan, 55000],
    'city': ['NYC', 'LA', 'NYC', 'SF', 'LA'],
    'target': [0, 1, 1, 0, 1]
})

numeric_features = ['age', 'salary']
categorical_features = ['city']

numeric_transformer = Pipeline([
    ('imputer', SimpleImputer(strategy='median')),
    ('scaler', StandardScaler())
])

categorical_transformer = Pipeline([
    ('imputer', SimpleImputer(strategy='most_frequent')),
    ('encoder', OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown='ignore'))
])

preprocessor = ColumnTransformer([
    ('num', numeric_transformer, numeric_features),
    ('cat', categorical_transformer, categorical_features)
])

pipeline = Pipeline([
    ('preprocessor', preprocessor),
    ('classifier', RandomForestClassifier(random_state=42))
])

X = data.drop('target', axis=1)
y = data['target']
pipeline.fit(X, y)
print(f"Pipeline trained successfully. Classes: {pipeline.classes_}")

Interactive practice lab

Write, save, and move through the mock interview

0/3 saved Step 1/3: Explain the concept
Ln 1, Col 1 1 lines 0 words 0.0 KB markdown

PRACTICE GUIDE

Explain the concept Demonstrate deep understanding by explaining the topic clearly, covering intuition, algorithms, and practical applications.

Explain the concept

Explain "Classical ML algorithms" as if teaching a senior engineer who is new to AI/ML. Cover the core intuition, when and why you would use it, and the key algorithms or techniques involved.

Goal

Demonstrate deep understanding by explaining the topic clearly, covering intuition, algorithms, and practical applications.

Guardrails

  • When would you choose logistic regression over a neural network?
  • Supervised learning
  • Unsupervised learning
  • Can select an appropriate algorithm family given the problem type and data shape.

Suggested Structure

  • Core intuition
  • Key algorithms or techniques
  • When to apply
  • Common variants
